title: HumorDB: Can AI understand graphical humor?
venue: 20th International Conference on Computer Vision, ICCV 2025 (Poster)
research_questions: Can AI systems understand graphical humor in images, as measured against human judgments?
How do state-of-the-art vision-only models and vision-language models compare with humans on binary humor classification, 1–10 funniness rating, and pairwise humor comparison?
Do minimally contrastive original/modified image pairs reveal whether models identify humor-inducing elements rather than superficial cues or editing artifacts?
Can model explanations, attention maps, and layer-wise analyses provide insight into how different architectures process visual humor?

datasets_used.name: HumorDB
datasets_used.size: 3,542 images; 1,271 pairs; 1,771 funny images and 1,771 not funny images

datasets_used.name: MSCOCO
datasets_used.size: 250 images in an external set of 650 non-humorous, non-edited images

datasets_used.name: Places365
datasets_used.size: 400 images in an external set of 650 non-humorous, non-edited images

methods_proposed.name: HumorDB
methods_proposed.summary: A curated visual humor dataset and benchmark containing photos, Photoshopped images, cartoons, sketches, and AI-generated images, with human measurements for binary, range-rating, and pairwise comparison tasks.

methods_proposed.name: Minimally contrastive visual humor pairs
methods_proposed.summary: The dataset includes 1,271 pairs of original humorous images and modified counterparts where the humor-inducing element is removed or altered while preserving visual similarity.

methods_proposed.name: Human-keyword-assisted VLM fine-tuning
methods_proposed.summary: For some fine-tuned vision-language runs, the authors prefixed prompts with words that appeared in at least 30% of human comparison-task responses for an image.

methods_proposed.name: Contrastive-pair attention analysis
methods_proposed.summary: For paired images, the paper approximates humorous regions using pixel-level differences between original and modified images and evaluates transformer attention maps with recall, strict box containment, and outside box ratio.

humor_theories.detail: The paper cites incongruity theory as the best-known theory of humor and motivates graphical humor as requiring expectations and identification of incongruities; it does not use a theory-specific annotation scheme.

evaluation_methods.detail: Online psychophysics experiments through Amazon Mechanical Turk and Prolific collected human labels for Binary, Range, and Comparison tasks; each participant rated 100 images, and each final image received 6-8 Binary or Range ratings or 4-6 comparisons.
VLM explanations were rated by humans on a 1–5 scale for identifying why an image is funny and for factual accuracy of the scene.

A separate in-lab validation study collected ratings for 400 images with >=5 ratings per image, yielding correlations with online data of rho = 0.78 for the binary task and rho = 0.72 for the range task.

Binary and Comparison tasks used accuracy against human-derived labels or majority votes; Range used RMSE against mean human ratings; control-set performance measured the percentage of external non-funny images classified as not funny.
Explanation alignment used All Words Score and Common Words Score based on stemming and synonym matching to human annotations.
Attention maps were evaluated with Recall, Strict Box Containment, and Outside Box Ratio; layer-wise logit attribution measured classification accuracy from each transformer layer.

headline_results.finding: The final HumorDB dataset is balanced and contains 3,542 images across 1,271 minimally contrastive pairs.
headline_results.value: 3,542 images; 1,271 pairs; 1,771 funny images; 1,771 not funny images

headline_results.finding: Human ratings validated the edits: original images were rated funnier than their modified counterparts for the majority of pairs.
headline_results.metric: original image rated funnier than modified counterpart

headline_results.finding: On the Range task, LLaVA with human-word fine-tuning achieved the best reported model RMSE on both test sets.
On the external non-humorous control set, the best reported model classified most images as not funny, supporting the claim that models were learning beyond editing artifacts.
headline_results.value: ViT-G14 (81%), LLaVA FT (80%), SwinV2 (76%), ViTHuge (71%), DinoV2 (62%)

headline_results.finding: In human evaluation of VLM explanations, Gemini received the highest ratings for both humor-source identification and factual accuracy.
Attention-map analysis showed limited localization of humorous regions even for transformer models.
headline_results.metric: attention Recall / Strict Box Containment / Outside Box Ratio
headline_results.value: ViTG-14: Recall Mean 0.265, SBC Mean 0.425, OBR Mean 0.348; DINOv2: Recall Mean 0.006, SBC Mean 0.552, OBR Mean 0.324

key_findings: HumorDB’s minimally contrastive pairs made the benchmark harder: the paper reports performance drops on testOnlyPairs and a 10% GPT-4o improvement when evaluating only original images, suggesting that modified images are an important control.

Pretraining mattered: pretrained vision models consistently outperformed non-pretrained models, while large vision-language models and fine-tuned LLaVA variants outperformed vision-only systems in the Binary task according to the paper’s reported trends.
The best Range-task model was LLaVA(words fine-tuned), with RMSE 1.68 ± 0.28 on testAllSet and 1.66 ± 0.31 on testOnlyPairs.

Image type strongly affected Binary performance: models were near chance on sketches, while Photoshopped and AI-generated images were easier; for example, Gemini-Flash scored 84 on Photoshopped images, 82 on AI-Gen images, and 53 on sketches.
VLM explanations could be fluent and sometimes aligned with human reasoning, but the paper shows cases where models hallucinated or missed the subtle distinction between paired images.
Attention maps and layer-wise analyses suggested that correct classification does not imply precise visual grounding: models often failed to focus on the small region that made the image funny.
limitations: The authors state that substantially enlarging HumorDB would likely improve performance but would require additional image modification, curation, and participant annotation.
Humor is culture-dependent, and the participant pool was predominantly from the United States (54%) and younger demographics (<40 years).
The benchmark uses majority votes and average ratings, which do not fully capture individual differences in humor perception.
Several original images came from the internet, and the paper notes that GPT-4o’s 10% improvement on original-only images suggests possible familiarity with similar images from training data.
The humorous region used for attention evaluation is approximated from pixel-level differences between original and modified image pairs, not from direct human segmentation.
safety_ethics_notes: The dataset construction removed images with explicit/offensive material and removed cases where humor relied on embedded text; the paper also notes cultural dependence and demographic skew in the participant pool.

artifacts.other: The supplementary material states that reference links for internet-collected images are provided in the repository.
